entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch1: The Stranger and the Ox
entries.text: At Mugwambi in Gandanzara, before Baba Johanne's birth, a mysterious stranger appeared. He asked for an ox to be slaughtered, ate it entirely in one day, buried the bones, and departed toward the east. That same day, heavy rains fell and his footprints vanished. This was the first sign before conception.

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch2: Mother Efi's Visions
entries.text: Mother Efi (Mai Efi Saziso) had visions while pregnant. She saw an angel descend with a shining dish containing a child white like snow. She received the child but both vanished like the blink of an eye. Her whole body became cold as ice. She was instructed not to tell the Roman priests or she would be killed.

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch3: Birth on October 1, 1914
entries.text: Baba Johanne was born on Thursday evening, October 1, 1914. At birth, miracles occurred: the house shook like an earthquake, dust fell from the ceiling, and a great rain came though it was not the rainy season. The child was named Shoniwa. From infancy he had mysterious episodes where he would collapse as if dead, then rise again.

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch5-6: Childhood and Youth
entries.text: As a child, Shoniwa did not follow other children. He would go alone to the hills, especially Mount Shena, breaking stones and shaping objects from sunrise to sunset. At about five years old, he testified being taken to the heavenly court where he saw and heard things not spoken among the living.

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch10: Descent of the Holy Spirit 1932
entries.text: On October 1, 1932, at Norton on Maitland Farm, the Holy Spirit descended upon Baba Johanne. He was struck by lightning while riding a bicycle and lay for sixteen days unable to speak. His companions Samson Mativera, Mazhambe, and Cheurombo cared for him. One night he was found outside though the door was barred. At dawn a voice from heaven called: 'Sixpence, arise!' He was commissioned at Chipukutu on Marimba Hill. He was 19 years old, having been born in 1914.

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch11: Marimba Hill Ministry
entries.text: After the Holy Spirit came, Baba Johanne went to Gomo reMarimba (Marimba Hill). Great crowds gathered. His singing staff (tsvimbo) was first heard there. At Chipukutu in Hunyani River, fish bit his fishing line like bees. Stars appeared to dance at night, and even trees were said to sing. Some compared him to Chaminuka the prophet, but others noted he walked on water without sinking and did not play mbira - his songs were of the church.

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch15: CID Interrogation and Trial
entries.text: After the Spirit came upon him, Baba Johanne was arrested at Norton. He was taken through seven places: Norton Police Station, Chegutu (Hartley), Kadoma, Harare, Marondera, Rusape, and Buhera. At his trial before Archbishop Cripps, Bishop Lulu from America, and Father O'hea of Kutama, the judges asked if he took money for healing. The people answered no. He was released with permission to continue his work.

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch22: Power Demonstrations
entries.text: Baba Johanne demonstrated divine power through many signs. A policeman named Chida came to arrest him and was struck blind, but after prayer his sight returned. Another policeman named Banda testified everywhere to what he saw. At Zaka in 1939, when a policeman beat the people, Baba Johanne flew like an eagle (gondo) carrying a young man named Richard Maiunda to safety on the hilltop.

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch26-27: Laws and Teachings (Mitemo)
entries.text: Baba Johanne established the Four Laws (Mitemo Mina) for his followers: 1) Love one another 2) Do not commit adultery 3) Do not kill or use violence 4) Do not practice witchcraft or consult witchdoctors. He taught that the body is the temple of God and must be kept pure. White garments represent purity, angels, and those who stand before the throne.

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch31-33: Port Elizabeth Ministry 1951
entries.text: On August 6, 1951, Baba Johanne arrived at Port Elizabeth in South Africa. The church grew rapidly among workers and migrants. Key women joined: Mai Megi, Mai Efi Manyathi, Mai Rose, and Mai Rhoda Kutsanzira. He had traveled there with Muvhangeri Saizi, Samuel Chinyanga, Sailos Nherera, and others. Port Elizabeth became a major center of the Masowe church.

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch51: Vision of Joshua 1:8 (1967)
entries.text: On August 14, 1967, while at Mutare, Baba Johanne went to the mountain to fast and pray. In a dream he went to heaven and was given the scroll of Joshua 1:8. It was revealed: 'Your people will be saved by the words in Joshua 1:8. They must keep the words of the Ten Commandments and the words of the prophets. Those who keep what is written in the Holy Bible will be saved. Their names will be found written in the Book of Life.'

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne: Vision of the New Land 1967
entries.text: On November 23, 1967, Baba Johanne was shown a new land (nyika itsva). He was instructed to send workers even to overseas countries, yet the root of the work remained in the South. He said: 'This new land will become the new home of truth and of the people of a new covenant.'

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch55-60: Travels to East Africa
entries.text: Baba Johanne traveled extensively through East Africa. He visited Nairobi, Kenya in 1963, where he spoke prophecies. He went to Dar es Salaam and Arusha in Tanzania. In 1964 he became ill starting from Dar es Salaam. Despite illness, he continued teaching about the spirit's journey after death and the resurrection.

entries.ref: Chronicles of Baba Johanne Ch64-68: Final Days in Zambia 1973
entries.text: In August 1973, Baba Johanne traveled from Arusha to Zambia. On 13 September 1973, he departed from the body at Ndola. On September 14, 1973, his words were read to the people at Ndola. On September 26, 1973, there was gathering in Southern Rhodesia. Silence came upon the people like a deep mist. There was weeping, yet not without understanding, for many said: 'He has finished what he was sent to do.'

entries.ref: Chronicles: Fourteen Workers Sent Forth
entries.text: Baba Johanne sent fourteen workers (warume gumi newana) to spread the message. These were his chosen evangelists who would carry the work to different nations. Each was given specific instructions and territories for their ministry.

entries.ref: Chronicles: The Prophecy of Black and White Bulls
entries.text: Baba Johanne prophesied: 'I saw two bulls fighting - a red bull and a black bull. The red bull represents the white people, the black bull represents the black people. In this land there will be war between white and black, but the whites will be defeated. They will flee, leaving their wealth, returning to their countries, but many will die. Your children will be taken from schools to war, many will die there. When you see war coming from Mozambique, that is when the whites will be defeated.'

entries.ref: Chronicles: Birth Date Confirmation - October 1, 1914
entries.text: AUTHORITATIVE DATE: Baba Johanne Masowe was born on October 1, 1914 (Musi wa 01-10-1914) at Gandanzara in the homestead of Mugwambi, Zimbabwe. He was NOT born in 1932 or 1933. The year 1932 marks the descent of the Holy Spirit when he was already 18 years old. The 1933 date refers to his release from prison on January 2, 1933.

entries.ref: Chronicles: The Names of Baba Johanne
entries.text: As the years passed, names gathered around him like garments. He was called Shoniwa (his birth name), Sixpence, Peter, Eria (Elijah), Johanne (John), Mangemba, and Harahwa. He declared that four of these were given from heaven and three from men. The name Johanne would stand above the rest. His lineage: Grandfather Mutambararo who died before completing a month as chief, succeeded by Masedza, who bore Jack, who bore Shoniwa.

entries.ref: Chronicles: The Singing Staff (Tsvimbo)
entries.text: At Marimba Hill, the singing staff (tsvimbo) that sang was first heard. This sacred staff would make musical sounds during worship. It became one of the signs of Baba Johanne's divine calling. When the staff sang, the people knew the Spirit was present.

entries.ref: Chronicles: Teaching on White Garments
entries.text: On October 4, 1955, Baba Johanne taught concerning the wearing of white garments. He said: 'White is the color of purity, of angels, and of those who stand before the throne. We wear white because we are a holy people set apart for God. The white robes are like those worn in heaven.' From that day, the white robes became the mark of his followers throughout Africa.

entries.ref: Chronicles: Teaching on Resurrection
entries.text: On March 15, 1955, Baba Johanne taught concerning the resurrection of the dead. He said: 'Death is not the end. The body returns to dust, but the spirit rises. Those who keep the commandments will rise to eternal life. I have seen the place prepared for the righteous - it shines brighter than the sun.'

entries.ref: Chronicles Ch13-14: Nyamweda and Chivero Ministry
entries.text: Baba Johanne walked from Marimba toward Nyamweda and Chivero. With him were witnesses including Samson Mativera, Mazhambe, Sylvester, and Enock Chakandamina. He prayed wherever he met those troubled in spirit, for he said he was sent to heal. At Nyamweda, he sang continually. A policeman named Chida came to arrest him and was struck blind. After Baba prayed, his sight returned. Another policeman named Banda testified everywhere to what he saw.

entries.ref: Chronicles: Bulawayo and Gweru Ministry
entries.text: From Masvingo, Baba Johanne traveled to Bulawayo with Muvhangeri Saizi, Samuel Chinyanga, Sailos Nherera, Muparidzi John Mupani, and Topias. At Bulawayo and Leighwoods, he found rest from persecution for a time. There were added to the fellowship: Mai Megi, Mai Efi Manyathi, Mai Rose, and Mai Rhoda Kutsanzira. From Bulawayo he went to Gweru, where he met Muparidzi George Marondera. He left his carpenter tools there, for he was a craftsman who made chairs and tables.

entries.ref: Chronicles: Johannesburg Ministry 1943-1944
entries.text: In 1943, Baba Johanne traveled to Johannesburg, South Africa. He stayed there for four months in 1944. The church grew among the workers and migrants in Johannesburg. Many were drawn to the message of the African Messiah. His ministry in South Africa laid foundations for what would become the major work in Port Elizabeth.

entries.ref: Chronicles: Botswana Ministry at Moroka
entries.text: Baba Johanne's ministry extended to Botswana (then Bechuanaland), particularly at Moroka. The message crossed borders as followers traveled between Zimbabwe, South Africa, and Botswana. The Masowe church established communities along these travel routes.

entries.ref: Chronicles: Lusaka Ministry Before Death
entries.text: In 1973, Baba Johanne was in Lusaka, Zambia. On December 2, 1961, workers had first gathered in Lusaka. By 1973, the work had grown throughout Zambia. Baba Johanne visited the communities before his final journey to Ndola.

entries.ref: Chronicles: Death and Funeral Procession 1973
entries.text: On 13 September 1973, Baba Johanne departed from the body at Ndola, Zambia. The funeral was a time of great mourning. On September 14, 1973, his final words were read to the people gathered at Ndola. On September 26, 1973, gatherings were held in Southern Rhodesia (Zimbabwe). The body was transported back to Zimbabwe for burial. Thousands came to pay respects to the African Messiah.

entries.ref: Chronicles: The Seven Places of Early Persecution
entries.text: After the Spirit came upon him, Baba Johanne was seized at Norton. He was taken to Norton Police Station, then transferred to Chegutu (Hartley), then to Kadoma, then to Harare, then to Marondera, then to Rusape, and finally to Buhera. These are the seven places of his suffering in the early days.

entries.ref: Chronicles: Malandelas and Early Persecution 1933
entries.text: In 1933, after his release from prison on January 2, 1933, Baba Johanne faced continued persecution. At Malandelas, people rose up against him with picks and shovels on August 15, 1933. Despite this opposition, the work continued to grow as more people were drawn to the message.

entries.ref: Chronicles: The Law Given at Sawe 1941
entries.text: On June 26, 1941, at Sawe, Baba Johanne gave the people the Law (Mutemo). This was the establishment of the Four Laws (Mitemo Mina) that would govern the Masowe community: Love one another, Do not commit adultery, Do not kill or use violence, Do not practice witchcraft. These laws became the foundation of the Masowe faith.
